key_facts:
Matter 7 (Newanforbi v. Candelaria, USDC E.D. Cal., federal §1983) arises from a November 1, 2024 welfare-check stop on I-580 East in Livermore, California at approximately 00:55 hours. Officers Santiago Meza-Gonzalez (#023295) and Melano (#023054) of CHP found plaintiff Brendan Ngehsi Newanforbi (CDCR #BF3571, DL F7953179, Class A) asleep in the driver's seat of a 2023 gray Tesla Model Y (plate 9EAX768 CA) parked on the right shoulder west of Isabel Avenue. No moving traffic violation was observed; the warrantless arrest was authorized under CVC §40300.5. Plaintiff is pre-diabetic, a condition that mimics signs of alcohol intoxication including red eyes, balance issues, and time perception errors.

Officer Meza served DS-367 (Age 21 and Older Officer's Statement, Case RS68845) designating a 'Chemical Test Refusal' and triggering administrative license suspension. The DS-367 contains multiple internal contradictions: (1) 'Chemical Test Refusal' is checked, yet blood was drawn at 07:38 AM on November 1, 2024 — approximately 6.5 hours post-arrest — pursuant to warrant WAR913372 approved by Judge A. Sekany at 02:34 AM; (2) the PAS section is entirely blank despite the admonition being given; (3) no driving was observed — the sole evidence of operation is plaintiff's own admission made while just woken from sleep; (4) Officer Meza's listed telephone bears a (915) area code (Texas) rather than a California prefix, indicating clerical deficiency; (5) FST field notes were destroyed after transfer to the report, raising potential spoliation; (6) FSTs referenced under Objective Symptoms but no SFST details (HGN, W&T, OLS) are recorded. Miranda rights were not administered, rendering all post-custody admissions (driving from Oakland, denying drinking) potentially suppressible. Plaintiff was booked at Santa Rita Jail (booking CAR913358) and was on active parole for PC §289(a)(1) at the time.

CHP subsequently issued a Detention Certificate under PC §849(b)(1) formally repudiating the arrest — a document that is legally equivalent to a finding that no crime was committed. No criminal charges were ever filed arising from the November 1, 2024 incident.

On December 4, 2024, DMV served a DS2044 Notification of Discovery (DSMS234, APS Case 34739340) disclosing five items: Driving Record Printout, Notice of Hearing, Notice of Stay, Officer's Statement DS-367, and Arrest/Incident Report(s). DMV witness C. Winston signed the notification on December 4, 2024. The notice stayed the suspension effective December 1, 2024 under VC §13353 pending hearing, and identified Diana Candelaria as the hearing officer. On December 9, 2024, plaintiff filed a Discovery Response asserting six legal grounds challenging the suspension and citing ten authorities including Terry v. Ohio, Mercer v. DMV, McDonnell v. DMV, Wong Sun, and Brown v. Illinois.

The APS hearing was held January 24, 2025 at 09:00 AM remotely via Microsoft Teams (Meeting ID 241846391884). On February 11, 2025, Hearing Officer Diana Candelaria issued her decision in Case 34739340 sustaining a one-year CDL suspension and commercial disqualification. Candelaria sustained the suspension despite: (1) CHP's own Detention Certificate under PC §849(b)(1) formally repudiating the arrest; (2) no criminal charges ever filed; (3) dashcam footage contradicting the DS-367 on material points; and (4) plaintiff's detailed legal arguments citing binding authority.

Candelaria's decision is constitutionally infirm on structural due process grounds. She served simultaneously as the prosecuting party and adjudicator — a dual role condemned as a due process violation in California DUI Lawyers Ass'n v. DMV and Knudsen v. DMV. She applied an irrebuttable presumption of official regularity to the DS-367, refused to address the Detention Certificate or the binding precedent of Mercer v. DMV, and issued a conclusory decision dismissing plaintiff's arguments as 'unpersuasive' without analysis. This structural error independently violates the Fourteenth Amendment under Mathews v. Eldridge and Bell v. Burson regardless of whether plaintiff can show prejudice on the merits. The CDL suspension and commercial disqualification caused direct economic harm including loss of Centerline CDL earnings (W-2 for 2024: $55,894) and ongoing commercial employment opportunity. The federal §1983 complaint was filed May 29, 2026.
procedural_status: November 1, 2024: CHP welfare-check stop on I-580 East, Livermore; custodial arrest of plaintiff; DS-367 served by Officer Meza (#023295) designating chemical test refusal; blood drawn pursuant to warrant WAR913372 (Judge A. Sekany, 02:34 AM); plaintiff booked at Santa Rita Jail (CAR913358). CHP subsequently issues Detention Certificate under PC §849(b)(1) repudiating the arrest; no criminal charges filed. December 1, 2024: CDL suspension effective under VC §13353, stayed pending APS hearing. December 4, 2024: DMV serves DS2044 Notification of Discovery (APS Case 34739340); five documents disclosed; hearing officer identified as Diana Candelaria; hearing scheduled for January 24, 2025 via Microsoft Teams. December 9, 2024: Plaintiff files Discovery Response asserting six legal grounds and ten case-law citations challenging the suspension. January 24, 2025: APS hearing held remotely (Microsoft Teams, Meeting ID 241846391884) before Hearing Officer Diana Candelaria. February 11, 2025: Candelaria issues APS Decision (Case 34739340) sustaining one-year CDL suspension and commercial disqualification; decision dismissed plaintiff's Detention Certificate and Mercer v. DMV arguments as 'unpersuasive' without analysis. May 29, 2026: Federal §1983 complaint filed in USDC E.D. Cal. (case number TBD).
evidence: CHP Detention Certificate (PC §849(b)(1)) — formally repudiates the November 1, 2024 arrest; legally equivalent to a finding that no crime was committed; Candelaria's refusal to address this document is the core due process violation

DS-367 (Officer Meza #023295, Case RS68845) — internally contradictory: 'Chemical Test Refusal' checked yet blood drawn at 07:38 AM; PAS section blank; no driving observed; (915) Texas area code anomaly; FST details absent despite Objective Symptoms notation
Blood draw warrant WAR913372 — approved by Judge A. Sekany at 02:34 AM on November 1, 2024; its existence directly contradicts the 'Chemical Test Refusal' designation on the DS-367
Blood test results — obtained pursuant to WAR913372; results are direct evidence of actual BAC at time of draw

CHP Arrest Report F7953179 (Officer Meza #023295, Citation RJ68845) — documents welfare-check basis for stop, no moving violation observed, warrantless arrest under CVC §40300.5, plaintiff's pre-diabetic condition, Miranda rights not administered
CHP Dashcam footage — contemporaneous video of the stop and field investigation; contradicts DS-367 on material points including plaintiff's observed condition and FST administration
FST field notes — destroyed after transfer to Arrest Report; potential spoliation; absence prevents independent verification of sobriety assessment

DS2044 Notification of Discovery (Case 34739340, signed C. Winston, December 4, 2024) — establishes the five documents DMV disclosed, confirms Candelaria as hearing officer, and identifies the stayed suspension under VC §13353

Respondent's Discovery Response (filed December 9, 2024) — six legal grounds challenging suspension; ten case-law citations including Mercer v. DMV, McDonnell v. DMV, Terry v. Ohio, Wong Sun, Brown v. Illinois; shows arguments were properly raised and then ignored

APS Decision (Case 34739340, February 11, 2025, Hearing Officer Diana Candelaria) — conclusory; dismisses Detention Certificate and binding Mercer v. DMV precedent as 'unpersuasive' without analysis; sustains one-year CDL suspension and commercial disqualification
Centerline CDL earnings records and W-2 (2024: $55,894; 2022: $39,553; 2023: $11,145) — document direct economic harm from CDL suspension and commercial disqualification
California DUI Lawyers Ass'n v. DMV — precedent condemning DMV hearing officers' dual prosecutor/adjudicator role as structural due process violation
Knudsen v. DMV — precedent directly on point condemning same structural bias at DMV APS proceedings
Mercer v. DMV — binding authority on effect of Detention Certificate in APS proceedings; Candelaria's refusal to apply this precedent is the clearest evidence of arbitrary decision-making
Bell v. Burson, 402 U.S. 535 (1971) — establishes due process requirements before license suspension; rebuts qualified immunity on clearly established law
Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319 (1976) — balancing test for procedural due process; structural bias satisfies the deprivation prong without showing prejudice
damages: $150,000 - $400,000+. Direct economic harm: one-year CDL suspension and commercial disqualification eliminating plaintiff's Class A commercial driving income (2024 W-2: $55,894; but-for earning capacity ~$72,000/year). Compensatory §1983 damages for constitutional deprivation of liberty interest in CDL. Punitive damages available against Candelaria individually for deliberate disregard of clearly established law (Knudsen, Mercer) and conscious application of a structurally biased adjudicatory process. Cascading harm: CDL loss cited as aggravating factor in November 2025 Discharge Review (Matter 8/15), compounding damages across litigation cluster.
risk: Qualified immunity is rebutted by clearly established law on multiple independent grounds: Bell v. Burson (1971) established 42 years before the violation that license suspension requires procedural due process; Mathews v. Eldridge established the governing balancing framework; Knudsen v. DMV and California DUI Lawyers Ass'n directly condemned the dual prosecutor/adjudicator role at DMV APS hearings as a due process violation — both were decided before February 2025. Candelaria cannot claim ignorance of precedent directly governing her role. The structural error doctrine, applied in the DMV context by Knudsen, may not require plaintiff to show prejudice on the merits — the structural bias itself is the constitutional violation. The DS-367's internal contradictions (refusal checked yet blood drawn; PAS blank; no driving observed) independently undermine the evidentiary basis for suspension and support an arbitrary-and-capricious theory. The Detention Certificate is the strongest single piece of evidence: CHP's own agency formally repudiated the arrest, and Candelaria's refusal to address it — dismissing it as 'unpersuasive' without analysis — is the clearest possible evidence of predetermined outcome and denial of a meaningful hearing.
